prompt: Develop an argument comparing systems of racial control in the New South and the trans-Mississippi West from 1877 to 1898.
student_response:
Racial control is when one group has control over another race and this was around in many parts of America. The New South was called new because the Civil War was over and the West was new because people kept finding land there. Both areas had many populations, although not always the same populations, and the government had to decide what to do.

In the South, people were rebuilding cities and farms that had been ruined. Some former workers left farms, some stayed, and other people moved into towns. There were elections but also arguments over who was allowed to be involved. The North sent southern soldiers to supervise for a while, and later those soldiers moved west. This made the South more modern, but also more traditional at the same time. Rules were different in every state and many people did not obey them. Violence was also a problem when groups disagreed.

The trans-Missisippi West covered a huge area. There were farmers, miners, cattle people, immigrants, and native tribes. They fought for water and land because the climate was dry. The government put groups in seperate areas so there would be less fighting, but there was still fighting. Some people were made citizens and other people were considered foreigners even when they were born there. The Spanish had built missions after the Civil War and these controlled western cities for the federal government.

There are many comparisons that could be made. The South had a warmer climate than most of the West, the West had more mountains, and both had railroads. Race effected where people could live and work, although class and gender mattered to. Sometimes control came from government and sometimes it did not. Eventually the areas became more equal, but this did not happen all at once. It is hard to say whether the systems were the same or different because each person had a different experiance and there were good and bad laws in both places.

feedback.thesis: The response does not make a defensible comparison of the two systems and explicitly declines to determine how they were similar or different.
feedback.contextualization: The opening supplies only generic descriptions of the regions rather than relevant broader historical context for racial control after 1877.
feedback.evidence: The essay offers broad references to elections, violence, land, and separated groups, but no sufficiently specific historical examples of racial-control systems in either region.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response earns the reasoning point because it organizes discussion around comparison, noting possible similarities and differences in government and nongovernment control, even though the comparison remains vague.

thesis: It is hard to say whether the systems were the same or different because each person had a different experiance and there were good and bad laws in both places.
contextualization: The New South was called new because the Civil War was over and the West was new because people kept finding land there.
evidence: There were elections but also arguments over who was allowed to be involved.
The government put groups in seperate areas so there would be less fighting, but there was still fighting.
analysis_reasoning: Sometimes control came from government and sometimes it did not.

feedback.thesis: The response does not make a clear, defensible claim comparing the two systems of racial control and ultimately refuses a comparison.
feedback.contextualization: The opening gives only vague descriptions of the postwar South and western land rather than meaningful broader context for racial control.
feedback.evidence: The essay provides no specific historical examples of racial-control systems in either region; its references to rules, separate areas, and violence remain unidentified.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: Although it lists surface similarities and differences, the response does not use comparison to explain how systems of racial control operated in the two regions.

thesis: It is hard to say whether the systems were the same or different because each person had a different experiance and there were good and bad laws in both places.
contextualization: The New South was called new because the Civil War was over and the West was new because people kept finding land there.
evidence: Rules were different in every state and many people did not obey them.
The government put groups in seperate areas so there would be less fighting, but there was still fighting.
analysis_reasoning: The South had a warmer climate than most of the West, the West had more mountains, and both had railroads.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the causes of Progressive reform from 1890 to 1920.
student_response:
By the late nineteenth century the United States had changed from a mostly rural society into one with giant corporations, crowded industrial cities, and millions of wage workers. Immigration supplied labor, railroads tied together a national market, and political machines often provided services while trading favors for votes. These developments left many Americans worried that older government was not able to deal with modern conditions.

There were many reformers with many complaints. Some worried about poverty and tenements, others about political corruption, alcohol, unsafe food, or the power of trusts. Middle-class professionals believed experts could study these problems. Workers wanted safer factories and shorter hours. Women who had experience in clubs and charitable work became active in public campaigns even though they could not yet vote nationally. Farmers also disliked railroad and banking power.

The Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire in 1911 killed workers who could not escape the building. Investigators and reformers discussed factory inspections and safety rules afterward. It was one of the famous events of the era. There were also elections, magazines, city investigations, and speeches about reform. Different states acted at different speeds and reform did not mean the same thing to everyone. Some reformers cared about democracy while others tried to control immigrants and the poor. Progressive reform came out of this whole collection of issues, leaders, and events, which makes the subject complicated and there is not really one explanation that fits it all.

feedback.thesis: The response argues that modern industrial and urban conditions produced many complaints that older government could not address, establishing a broad causal line.
feedback.contextualization: The opening accurately frames reform amid corporations, industrial cities, wage labor, immigration, railroads, and political machines.
feedback.evidence: The Triangle fire and ensuing safety discussion, political-machine practices, women's club activity, trusts, tenements, and worker demands are used as examples of pressures for reform.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response uses causation by connecting varied industrial-era problems and constituencies to reform, but it does not develop a sufficiently sophisticated or evidence-rich explanation for the second point.

thesis: These developments left many Americans worried that older government was not able to deal with modern conditions.
Progressive reform came out of this whole collection of issues, leaders, and events
contextualization: By the late nineteenth century the United States had changed from a mostly rural society into one with giant corporations, crowded industrial cities, and millions of wage workers.
evidence: The Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire in 1911 killed workers who could not escape the building.
Investigators and reformers discussed factory inspections and safety rules afterward.
Women who had experience in clubs and charitable work became active in public campaigns even though they could not yet vote nationally.
analysis_reasoning: Some worried about poverty and tenements, others about political corruption, alcohol, unsafe food, or the power of trusts.

feedback.thesis: The response lists many complaints but does not establish a specific causal claim or line of reasoning about which factors produced Progressive reform.
feedback.contextualization: The opening accurately frames reform through industrialization, urbanization, immigration, national markets, and political machines.
feedback.evidence: The Triangle fire is one specific example, but the rubric requires at least two specific examples for the evidence point.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response uses causal reasoning by linking modern industrial and urban problems, reform constituencies, and the Triangle fire to demands for reform.

thesis: Progressive reform came out of this whole collection of issues, leaders, and events, which makes the subject complicated and there is not really one explanation that fits it all.
contextualization: By the late nineteenth century the United States had changed from a mostly rural society into one with giant corporations, crowded industrial cities, and millions of wage workers.
evidence: The Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire in 1911 killed workers who could not escape the building.
analysis_reasoning: Investigators and reformers discussed factory inspections and safety rules afterward.

prompt: Develop an argument evaluating continuity and change in federal restrictions on civil liberties during wartime from 1917 to 1945.
student_response:
Before the twentieth century, the government had limited speech in earlier emergencies, such as the Alien and Sedition laws and Lincoln's suspension of habeas corpus during the Civil War. World war again raised the question of whether individual rights survived a national crisis. From 1917 to 1945 federal restrictions remained continuous because officials punished people considered dangerous during both wars, but the main focus changed from political dissent in World War I toward groups treated as enemy races in World War II.

In World War I, Congress enacted the Espionage Act of 1917. It could be used against people who interfered with the draft or opposed the war. Newspapers and speakers had to be careful and the Post Office could keep certain publications out of the mail. The Supreme Court passed a rule about dangerous speech, it meant normal speech could become illegal in wartime. A person might be punished even without directly helping an enemy. People disagreed about whether this protected soldiers or just protected leaders from criticism, and the answer was not clear during the emergency.

During the second war the government was less focused on ordinary antiwar speeches but more focused on people's family backgrounds. Officials removed families from communities without individually proving that each person had done something wrong. Property and freedom could be lost. The policy was described as military safety. There were also investigations, loyalty worries, and many rumors, citizens did not always know which rumors were accurate.

Civil liberties include speech, privacy, fair trials, and equal treatment. Not every liberty was restricted in the same way. Elections still happened, newspapers still printed stories, and citizens continued to criticize officials, so the whole country was not exactly a dictatorship. At the same time, being at war made restrictions easier for the public to accept.

The years from the first war to the second war contained both kinds of federal action. Some restrictions were about words and opinions, some were about who officials thought might be dangerous. Fear was present and the government had more power. The details changed, but civil liberties were under pressure whenever leaders said national safety was involved.

feedback.thesis: The thesis identifies continuity in wartime punishment and a change from suppressing dissent to targeting people by perceived enemy ancestry.
feedback.contextualization: The essay situates the period within earlier emergency restrictions by referencing the Alien and Sedition laws and Civil War suspension of habeas corpus.
feedback.evidence: The Espionage Act, draft opposition, postal restrictions, wartime speech rules, and removal of families without individual proof support the continuity-and-change argument.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response sustains continuity and change while adding nuance about different liberties, continued elections and criticism, and the distinction between repression and total dictatorship.

thesis: From 1917 to 1945 federal restrictions remained continuous because officials punished people considered dangerous during both wars, but the main focus changed from political dissent in World War I toward groups treated as enemy races in World War II.
contextualization: Before the twentieth century, the government had limited speech in earlier emergencies, such as the Alien and Sedition laws and Lincoln's suspension of habeas corpus during the Civil War.
evidence: In World War I, Congress enacted the Espionage Act of 1917.
the Post Office could keep certain publications out of the mail.
Officials removed families from communities without individually proving that each person had done something wrong.
analysis_reasoning: Elections still happened, newspapers still printed stories, and citizens continued to criticize officials, so the whole country was not exactly a dictatorship.

feedback.thesis: The thesis clearly identifies continuity in wartime punishment and change from political dissent to racialized suspicion.
feedback.contextualization: Alien and Sedition laws and Lincoln's suspension of habeas corpus provide relevant pre-1917 context.
feedback.evidence: The Espionage Act, draft opposition, postal restrictions, dangerous-speech doctrine, and family removals support the stated continuity and change.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The essay structures its argument through continuity and change, but its limited identification and development of World War II evidence does not demonstrate sufficiently sophisticated understanding for the second point.

thesis: From 1917 to 1945 federal restrictions remained continuous because officials punished people considered dangerous during both wars, but the main focus changed from political dissent in World War I toward groups treated as enemy races in World War II.
contextualization: Before the twentieth century, the government had limited speech in earlier emergencies, such as the Alien and Sedition laws and Lincoln's suspension of habeas corpus during the Civil War.
evidence: In World War I, Congress enacted the Espionage Act of 1917.
Officials removed families from communities without individually proving that each person had done something wrong.
analysis_reasoning: The details changed, but civil liberties were under pressure whenever leaders said national safety was involved.

prompt: Develop an argument evaluating continuity and change in Indigenous diplomatic strategies toward European colonies from 1607 to 1754.
student_response:
European colonies entered a continent already occupied by numerous Indigenous nations with their own trade routes, rivalries, and political traditions. Because English, French, Dutch, and Spanish settlements competed for land and commerce, Native leaders could sometimes use imperial rivalry to defend autonomy. From 1607 to 1754 Indigenous diplomacy continuously sought advantageous alliances and a balance among outsiders, but it changed as settler population and land pressure increased, making broader Native coordination and the threat of military resistance more urgent than the smaller exchanges of the early colonial period.

Diplomacy could take many forms. Leaders met, exchanged gifts, delivered speeches, selected interpreters, and carried messages back to their communities. A meeting was not only two individuals talking. Each speaker represented relatives and other leaders who might reject an agreement. Words also had to be translated, and a translated word might not carry exactly the same meaning. Agreements could concern trade, travel, land, prisoners, or military help. Everybody wanted the same European products, so every Native community always made the same diplomatic choice.

Ceremony mattered. Participants might remember an older promise before talking about a new dispute. Gifts showed respect but could also create an obligation. Colonial officials sometimes wanted a written paper with a fixed boundary. Indigenous speakers could emphasize an ongoing relationship that needed to be renewed. This difference created confusion. Sometimes the confusion was probably accidental and sometimes a negotiator used it for an advantage.

The Covenant Chain was one diplomatic arrangement involving the Haudenosaunee and English colonies. It was discussed in councils and represented as a relationship that required attention. Different participants could understand the arrangement differently. It is one example of Native people appearing as diplomats rather than simply reacting to whatever colonists decided.

Leaders had other choices as well. They might delay an answer, send a new delegation, move a community, allow some trade, reject a missionary, or warn that warfare could follow. Neutrality could be a strategy. So could alliance. So could refusing to commit until it became clearer which outsider offered better terms. A leader who made a successful agreement could gain influence, while a failed agreement could lead to criticism at home.

Diplomacy did not mean equality. Colonists wanted land and traders wanted profit. Native communities wanted security but did not all define security in the same way. Some lived near colonies and others farther away. Some had enemies who seemed like a greater immediate danger than Europeans. All tribes joined together whenever Europeans threatened one of them, although at other times they disagreed.

Indigenous diplomacy from 1607 to 1754 therefore included meetings, ceremonies, alliances, threats, and efforts at neutrality. The methods could overlap, and an agreement might work for a time without solving every conflict. There was continuity and change during these years, but the many communities and local situations make the details difficult to put into one pattern.

feedback.thesis: The thesis clearly identifies continuity in balancing outsiders and change toward broader coordination and threatened resistance as land pressure grew.
feedback.contextualization: The response establishes Indigenous political traditions and trade networks and explains how competing European empires created diplomatic opportunities.
feedback.evidence: Gift exchange, interpreters, councils, boundaries, the Covenant Chain, neutrality, alliances, delegations, trade, and threats provide multiple concrete examples of diplomatic strategy.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: Continuity and change structure the response, but broad unsupported claims and limited evidence for the asserted shift toward broader coordination prevent complex understanding.

thesis: From 1607 to 1754 Indigenous diplomacy continuously sought advantageous alliances and a balance among outsiders, but it changed as settler population and land pressure increased, making broader Native coordination and the threat of military resistance more urgent than the smaller exchanges of the early colonial period.
contextualization: European colonies entered a continent already occupied by numerous Indigenous nations with their own trade routes, rivalries, and political traditions.
evidence: Leaders met, exchanged gifts, delivered speeches, selected interpreters, and carried messages back to their communities.
The Covenant Chain was one diplomatic arrangement involving the Haudenosaunee and English colonies.
Neutrality could be a strategy. So could alliance.
analysis_reasoning: Indigenous diplomacy from 1607 to 1754 therefore included meetings, ceremonies, alliances, threats, and efforts at neutrality.

feedback.thesis: The thesis defensibly identifies continuity in alliance balancing and change toward broader coordination and threatened resistance under greater land pressure.
feedback.contextualization: The opening situates diplomacy among preexisting Native nations and competing English, French, Dutch, and Spanish settlements.
feedback.evidence: The Covenant Chain is the only specific historical example; generic ceremonies, gifts, messages, neutrality, alliances, and threats do not meet the two-example threshold.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response uses continuity-and-change language and compares diplomatic methods, but the body does not concretely trace the claimed change over time.

thesis: From 1607 to 1754 Indigenous diplomacy continuously sought advantageous alliances and a balance among outsiders, but it changed as settler population and land pressure increased, making broader Native coordination and the threat of military resistance more urgent than the smaller exchanges of the early colonial period.
contextualization: European colonies entered a continent already occupied by numerous Indigenous nations with their own trade routes, rivalries, and political traditions.
evidence: The Covenant Chain was one diplomatic arrangement involving the Haudenosaunee and English colonies.
analysis_reasoning: Indigenous diplomacy from 1607 to 1754 therefore included meetings, ceremonies, alliances, threats, and efforts at neutrality.
